sections.title: A: Review Vowel Sounds and Digraphs

steps.text: Tutor sets out all the tiles taught so far.
In this book, we'll always start by reviewing the vowel sounds. Which ones are the vowels?
Student taps A, E, I, O, U.
How did you know?
Expected: They're yellow.
Go ahead and tap each vowel.
Student taps Apple Apple /a/ /a/ /a/, etc.
Tutor points to CH tile.
Why are there 2 letters on 1 tile?
Expected: Because those 2 letters make just 1 sound.
That's right. Two letters that make just 1 sound are called Digraphs. Show me the other digraphs.
Student points to the TH, SH, CK, and WH tiles.
Point to each Digraph and tell me the sound it makes.
Student points to each Digraph and makes its sound.
Let's take a look at your extra practice page.
Go ahead and read the words to me.
Review extra practice page for accuracy. If student has difficulty, repeat the last lesson.
Now I'll say a nonsense word, and you repeat it. Ready? ZOND.
Repeat that word.
Student: Zond
Now say just the FIRST sound in that word.
Point to the tile that makes that sound.
Student points to the Z tile.
Dictate: DOPT, SUMP, MAND, VISK
Now we're going to switch. This time, I want the LAST sound. Ready? HAMP.
What's the LAST sound?
Point to the tile that makes that sound.
Student points to the P tile.
Dictate: PEND, DAPT, BELF
Now for the MIDDLE sound—the vowel. Say both the sound AND its keyword. Ready? THANT.
What's the MIDDLE sound?
What keyword starts with /a/?
Student: Apple
Point to that tile.
Student points to the A tile.
Dictate: GUSK, BIST, CHOFT, THEPT

sections.title: D: NEW TEACHING #1: Read Words with Blends

steps.text: Let's start working on words.
Touch-and-say each sound.
Student uses index finger to touch SH and say /sh/, touch I and say /i/, touch P and say /p/.
Slowly blend those sounds together.
Student: /shhhhh/-/iiii/-/p/
Now say it like a word.
Student: Ship
Is there a Blend in that word?
Student: No
Is there a Digraph in that word?
Student: Yes, the SH is a digraph; those 2 letters make only 1 sound.
In the last lesson, where were the Blends? At the beginning of a word or at the end?
Student: End
Tutor removes SH, drags in S and L.
As you can see, Blends can also be at the beginning of a word. Touch-and-say these sounds, then blend them into a word.
Student uses index finger to touch S and say /s/, touch L and say /l/, touch I and say /i/, touch P and say /p/. Then student slowly blends, /ssss/-/llll/-/iiii/-/p/, then says it like a word: SLIP.
Tutor removes I, drags in A.
What is that word now?
Student: SLAP
Tutor removes S, drags in F.
What is that word now?
Student: FLAP
And where's the Blend?
Student: At the beginning the F-L
Tutor builds words: SLICK, FLIP, GRIP, SLIM, FLOP, GRIM, SLAM, PROP, TRIM, CLAM, PLOP, BRIM.

sections.title: E: NEW TEACHING #2: Read Words with Blends

steps.text: There is no New Teaching #2 in this lesson. Proceed to Part F.

sections.title: F: Spell Real Words with Fingers, then Tiles

steps.text: Now it's your turn to build words with tiles. The first word is SWAM. Repeat that word.
Student: Swam
Now say it again, very slowly.
Student: /ssss/-/w/-/aaaa/-/mmmm/
Use your fingers to break it into sounds.
Student uses NON-WRITING hand and raises thumb while saying /s/, raises index finger while saying /w/, raises middle finger while saying /a/, and raises ring finger while saying /m/.
Pull down a tile for each finger, while making its sound.
To double-check, slowly run your finger below those tiles while blending those sounds together.
Student: /ssss/-/w/-/aaaa/-/m/
And say it fast like a word.
Student: Swam
Is that the word you wanted?
Student: Yes
Now change just one letter... to make that word say SWIM.
Student says SWIM slowly, finger spells it, drags out A, drags in I, then double checks by blending.
Nice work. Put those tiles away and let's try another word.
Dictate: DRUG, DRAG, PLUM, PLUG, SPIN, SPIT, FLOP, FLAP, SLED, SPED, FLASH, FLESH.

sections.title: G: Read Nonsense Words on Tiles

steps.text: Now I'm going to build a nonsense word.
Touch-and-say each sound.
Student touches G and says /g/, touches R and says /r/, touches A and says /a/, touches T and says /t/.
Slowly blend those sounds together.
Student slowly drags his index finger in a curve below the word while saying /g/-/rrrr/-/aaaa/-/t/.
Now say it fast like a word.
Student quickly drags his index finger below the word while saying GRAT.
Beautiful. Put those tiles away.
Tutor builds: STIM, DROB, GLEZ, BLUN, DRIN.

sections.title: H: Spell Nonsense Words with Fingers, then Tiles

steps.text: Now you'll spell some nonsense words. Ready? The first word is STIG.
Repeat that word.
Student: Stig
Say it slowly.
Student: /ssss/-/t/-/iiii/-/g/
Use your fingers to break it into sounds.
Student raises thumb and says /s/, raises index finger and says /t/, raises middle finger and says /i/, then raises ring finger and says /g/.
Pull down a tile for each of those sounds.
Double-check by blending those sounds together.
Student drags index finger below the tiles while slowly blending the sounds together.
Now fast like a word.
Student: Stig
Is that the word you wanted?
Student: Yes
Excellent. Put those tiles back.
Dictate: BLAN, GROT, PLUB, SPESH.

sections.title: I: Read Words Through Word Frame

steps.text: Now you're going to read words using a word frame.
Tutor gives the Lesson 2: Read These Words page and the word frame to the student.
If you know that word for sure, just say it.
Otherwise, touch-and-say each sound, then blend them together.
Student either says word, or touches and says each sound, slowly blends them together, then says it fast like a word.
Nice. Now move down to the next word.

sections.title: J: Spell Words with Fingers, then Paper

steps.text: Now you're going to spell words on paper.
Tutor hands student a piece of paper and a pencil.
First you'll do finger spelling. Ready? The word is FLIP.
Repeat that word.
Student: Flip
Say it again, slowly.
Student: /ffff/-/llll/-/iiii/-/p/
Use your fingers to break it into sounds.
Student raises thumb on NON-WRITING hand while saying /f/, raises index finger while saying /l/, raises middle finger while saying /i/, raises ring finger while saying /p/.
Now make each sound as you write the letter.
Student says /f/ and writes F, says /l/ and writes L, says /i/ and writes I, says /p/ and writes P.
As a last step, double-check what you wrote.
To do that, first blend the sounds together.
Student: /fffff/-/llll/-/iiii/-/p/
Now say it fast like a word.
Student: Flip
Is that the word you wanted?
Student: Yes
Dictate Real Words: TROT, DRAG, STEM, BRUSH
Dictate Nonsense Words: SNIB, GROD, SMUP, BLEM, STAZ

sections.title: K: Review Sight Word Reading Deck

steps.text: Let's review the Sight Word Reading Deck we created last time.
Tutor pulls out the Sight Word Reading Deck and gives to student.
Put your thumb over the clue and read the word.
If you're not sure you said the right word, lift up your thumb and look at your clue. Try it.
Student puts thumb over clue and reads each word out loud.
Now that you're warmed up, keep the clue hidden and read them again... as fast as you can.
Student puts thumb over clue, flips through the deck as fast as he can, reading each word out loud.
Good reading. Now give me the deck.
Tutor sorts the deck.
Now that I've sorted the deck into a different order, let's go through it again. I'll try to flip the cards a little faster than you did.
Student reads each word out loud.
Nice. Now I'll sort them one more time, then we'll try 'supersonic' speed.
Tutor sorts the cards, then flashes them as rapidly as possible.
Excellent. That's it for Sight Words for today.
Tutor puts the Sight Word Reading Deck away.

sections.title: L: Review Sight Word Spelling Cards

steps.text: Let's review the spelling of the tricky Sight words.
Tutor pulls out the Sight Word Spelling Cards and holds up the first card.
What's this word?
Student reads the word.
Why is that letter in red?
Expected: Because it's the tricky letter; it doesn't sound like that, it's silent, etc.
Stare at that card until you've got a good picture of it in your mind, and you can see it with your eyes closed. Tell me when you're ready.
Student: I'm ready.
Tutor removes Sight Word Spelling Card from view.
Look at that blank wall and see that word and its red letter. Can you see it?
Student: Yes
How do you spell (sight word here)? When you get to the red letter, say it louder than the rest.
Student looks at a blank wall, 'sees' the word, and spells it out loud. Student says red letter louder than the rest.
Do that again.
Student repeats the 'see and spell' process.
Good. Now using your index and middle finger as the pen, write (sight word here) on the desk.
Student 'writes' the word on the desktop using big arm movements... making the red letter extra loud.
Do it again.
Student repeats the 'see and write' step on the table.
As a last step, write (sight word here) on paper, saying each letter as you write it. Then underline the red letter.
Student writes the sight word on paper, saying each letter out loud and making the red letter extra loud. Student then underlines the red letter.
One more time.
Student repeats the 'see and write' step on paper.
Now you'll read some phrases... that tell Who, Did What, or Where.
Tutor pulls out the Lesson 2: Read These Phrases page and covers all but the Who phrases.
This group of phrases tell WHO. Read each phrase to me.
Student reads each WHO phrase out loud.
A phrase... is not a complete sentence. It takes at least TWO phrases... to be a sentence. So let's look at the next set of phrases.
Tutor lowers the blank card to expose the Did What phrases.
These phrases tell DID WHAT. Read each phrase to me.
Student reads each DID WHAT phrase out loud.
A sentence must have a WHO and a DID WHAT phrase.
Go ahead and pick a WHO and a DID WHAT phrase and make a short sentence.
Student verbally says a short sentence, such as 'Glen had a snack.'
Nice. A phrase can also tell WHERE something happened.
Tutor lowers the blank card to expose the Where phrases.
Read each WHERE phrase to me.
Student reads each WHERE phrase out loud.
Now read the ADD-ON phrases.
Student reads each ADD-ON phrase out loud.
Create a sentence using a phrase from each category.
Student verbally creates a sentence such as 'Bob Smith stuck a drum on a flat rock and had fun.'

sections.title: N: Spell Phrases and Sight Words on Paper

steps.text: Now you're going to write three phrases.
Tutor hands student a piece of paper and a pencil.
The first phrase is DROP THE FROG. Repeat that phrase.
Student: Drop the frog.
Go ahead and write that phrase. Use finger spelling to help with the tough words.
Student works on his own with no help from the tutor.
Double check by reading exactly what you wrote.
Student looks at his paper while reading the phrase.
Any word that you want help checking?
Tutor scans written phrase for errors.
That's perfect. Here's the next phrase.
Dictate: STEP ON A TWIG, GRAB THE FLAG.
Tutor dictates each Sight Word from the Sight Word Spelling deck.
Now let's look at some sentences.
Tutor pulls out the Lesson 2: Read These Sentences page.
Read that first sentence to yourself.
Student reads sentence to himself.
Now read it out loud.
Student: Glen did drag a sled up the hill with Greg.
Now mark each phrase. Mark the WHO phrase.
Student uses pencil to draw under-up Glen.
Mark the DID WHAT phrase.
Student uses pencil to draw under-up did drag a sled.
Mark the WHERE phrase.
Student uses pencil to draw under-up up the hill.
Mark the ADD-ON phrase.
Student uses pencil to draw under-up with Greg.
Now read that sentence with phrasing. That means put a small pause... between each phrase.
Student: Glen... did drag a sled... up the hill... with Greg.
Nice. Now read sentence number 3.
Have your student read and mark each ODD-numbered sentence.
Now you're going to write three sentences.
Tutor hands student a piece of paper and a pencil.
The first sentence is GET FRAN TO GRAB THE FLAG. Repeat that sentence.
Student: Get Fran to grab the flag.
Go ahead and write that sentence. Use finger spelling to help with any tough words.
Student works on his own with no help from the tutor.
Check your sentence by reading exactly what you wrote.
Student looks at his paper while reading the sentence.
Here's the first question I'm always going to ask: Does it start with a capital and end with punctuation?
Student: Yes (or else student fixes sentence).
Any word that you want help checking?
Tutor scans written sentence for errors and uses guided discovery as needed.
That's perfect. Here's the next sentence.
Dictate: DID STAN DROP THE FROG IN THE POND?, GLEN DID STEP ON A TWIG AND HIT HIS HAND.
Now you're ready to read a story.
Tutor pulls out the Lesson 2: Read This Story #1 page and hands it to the student. Tutor also gives student a 5x8 index card to use as a marker.
Preread the story. That means read it to yourself first. Sound out any words you're not sure of. Then you'll read it out loud to me.
Student reads the story out loud to tutor (or retells it in their own words). Tutor asks comprehension questions.
